questions.question: What is the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
options: Forward proxies use HTTP; reverse proxies use HTTPS
A forward proxy sits in front of clients; a reverse proxy sits in front of servers
Forward proxies cache responses; reverse proxies do not
A reverse proxy is just a forward proxy running in the opposite direction
questions.explanation: A forward proxy is configured by the client and speaks to the internet on the client's behalf (corporate filtering, anonymization). A reverse proxy is configured by the server operator and receives client traffic on the servers' behalf (Nginx, Cloudflare). Clients typically don't know a reverse proxy exists.

questions.question: How does a browser tell a forward proxy which server to connect to?
options: It sends a CONNECT request with the target hostname
It sends the full URL (http://host/path) in the HTTP request line instead of just the path
It adds a Proxy-Destination header
It opens a second TCP connection to the target and tunnels through the proxy
questions.explanation: When a browser is configured to use a proxy, it changes the request from `GET /path HTTP/1.1` to `GET http://example.com/path HTTP/1.1`. The full URL in the request line tells the proxy where to connect. For HTTPS, browsers use the CONNECT method to establish a tunnel.

questions.question: Why must the proxy rewrite `GET http://example.com/page HTTP/1.1` to `GET /page HTTP/1.1` before forwarding to the origin?
options: The full URL form is invalid HTTP — the origin server will reject it
The origin server expects only the path; it already knows its own hostname
Including the full URL causes the origin to redirect to itself
HTTP/1.1 forbids the http:// scheme in request lines
questions.explanation: The origin server knows it's example.com — it doesn't need the scheme and host in the request line. The absoluteURI form (full URL) is only understood by proxies. Sending it to the origin server will result in a 400 Bad Request from most servers.

questions.question: What are hop-by-hop headers and why must the proxy strip them?
options: Headers that contain binary data encoded in hops
Headers like Connection and Keep-Alive that apply only to the immediate connection, not end-to-end
Headers added by CDN edge nodes as the request hops across regions
Headers with a TTL field that expires after each proxy hop
questions.explanation: Hop-by-hop headers (Connection, Keep-Alive, Transfer-Encoding, Upgrade) describe the current TCP connection, not the end-to-end request. If a proxy forwards them, the origin might misinterpret the connection state. RFC 7230 requires proxies to consume and strip these headers.

questions.question: The proxy returns 502 to the client. What does this mean?
options: The client sent a malformed request
The proxy refused to handle the request because the host is blocked
The proxy connected to the origin but got an invalid or no response
The requested resource was not found on the origin server
questions.explanation: 502 Bad Gateway means the server acting as a proxy or gateway received an invalid response from the upstream server. The proxy successfully accepted the client's request but something went wrong talking to the origin: connection refused, timeout, or a garbage response.
